metadata.summary: Ownership eliminates memory errors at compile time. One owner per value; transfers on assignment. References borrow temporarily. Answer three questions before writing function bodies.

content.title: Rust Ownership Model
content.summary: Ownership eliminates memory errors at compile time. One owner per value; transfers on assignment. References borrow temporarily. Answer three questions before writing function bodies.

why.p: Memory safety without GC is only achievable because the compiler enforces ownership rules statically. Every clone() added to silence the borrow checker is a sign the data layout or function signature is wrong, not that the checker is wrong. Fighting the borrow checker with .clone() or Rc<RefCell<T>> costs runtime performance and signals a design flaw.

list.item: Any Rust project — ownership rules are always in effect.
Designing function signatures (consume vs borrow vs share decision is upfront).
Translating algorithms from GC languages (Python, Go, JS) to Rust.
Refactoring code that fights the borrow checker with .clone() or Rc<RefCell<T>>.
Writing async code where 'static and Send + Sync bounds appear.
Tiny scripts where .clone() everywhere is acceptable — borrow-checker design costs more than it saves.
Procedural macros — token stream manipulation has different ownership idioms.
Wrapping C APIs via FFI — manual lifetime management dominates; standard ownership advice barely applies.
Code generated from schemas (Prost, sqlx) — accept the generated ownership shape.

rule.statement: Each value in Rust has exactly one owner at any time.
rule.rationale: Enforces single-point responsibility for deallocation; when the owner goes out of scope the value is dropped.

rule.statement: Assignment of a non-Copy type moves ownership; the original binding becomes invalid.
rule.rationale: Prevents double-free and use-after-free at compile time with zero runtime cost.

rule.statement: Copy types (all stack-only primitives like i32, bool, char, f64, and tuples of Copy types) are copied on assignment; both bindings remain valid.
rule.rationale: Copy is safe only when bitwise duplication is semantically correct and carries no heap allocation.

example.title: Move vs Copy Semantics
example.note: String is not Copy (heap allocation); i32 is Copy (stack-only).

rule.statement: At any given time you may have either any number of immutable references (&T) OR exactly one mutable reference (&mut T), but not both.
rule.rationale: Shared XOR mutable — eliminates data races statically.

rule.statement: References must not outlive the value they point to.
rule.rationale: Dangling pointers are a compile error, not a runtime crash.

example.title: Multiple Immutable Borrows
Conflicting Borrow Rules
example.note: Rearrange so r1/r2 are last used before r3 is introduced — Non-Lexical Lifetimes (NLL) may allow it if r1/r2 are not used after r3's introduction.

rule.statement: Add explicit lifetime parameters only when the compiler asks; name them after their meaning ('src, 'cfg) not positionally ('a, 'b).
rule.rationale: Lifetime elision handles the common cases; premature explicit lifetimes obscure intent and cascade into callers.

example.title: Lifetime Patterns

rule.statement: For async code, all values moved into spawned tasks must be 'static — capture by value with move or Arc::clone shared state.
rule.rationale: tokio::spawn and std::thread::spawn require 'static + Send; borrowed locals do not satisfy this.

section.name: Smart Pointers: Box, Rc, RefCell, Arc, Mutex
Box — Heap Allocation with Single Ownership

rule.statement: Use Box<T> for recursive types (where the size is unknown at compile time) or to move large values to the heap.
rule.rationale: Box provides heap allocation with ownership semantics identical to stack values; the pointer is the owner and frees on drop.

example.title: Box for Recursive Types

rule.statement: Use Rc<T> for shared ownership in single-threaded code only; Rc is not Send.
rule.rationale: Rc uses non-atomic reference counting — cheaper than Arc but unsafe across threads.

example.title: Rc Reference Counting

rule.statement: Use RefCell<T> when you need mutability inside an otherwise-immutable context; borrow rules are checked at runtime, not compile time.
rule.rationale: Moves the borrow-check to runtime — a panic replaces a compile error. Use only as a last resort when ownership redesign is truly impossible.

rule.statement: Do not reach for Rc<RefCell<T>> to silence the borrow checker — it usually means the data layout is wrong (graph forced into a tree, etc.).
rule.rationale: Runtime borrow panics are harder to debug than compile errors and add pointer-chasing overhead.

section.name: Arc and Mutex — Thread-Safe Shared State

rule.statement: Use Arc<T> (atomic reference counting) to share ownership across threads. Combine with Mutex<T> for shared mutation.
rule.rationale: Arc is Send + Sync; Rc is not. Atomic refcount is more expensive than Rc — prefer message passing (mpsc) over Arc<Mutex<T>> when possible.

rule.statement: In async code use tokio::sync::Mutex when you need to hold the lock across an .await point; use std::sync::Mutex for compute-heavy synchronous critical sections.
rule.rationale: std::sync::Mutex is not async-aware — holding it across .await blocks a thread. tokio::Mutex yields the task instead.

example.title: Arc and Mutex for Thread-Safe State

rule.statement: Accept the most permissive type: &str over &String, &[T] over &Vec<T>, impl AsRef<Path> over &PathBuf.
rule.rationale: Callers can always coerce to the permissive type; accepting the concrete type forces unnecessary allocations at call sites.

rule.statement: Return owned data when the function allocates; return a reference when slicing into a parameter.
rule.rationale: Clarifies lifetime semantics at the function boundary and avoids forcing callers to clone.

rule.statement: Derive Copy only for types that are purely stack-allocated (all fields are Copy). Derive Clone for types that need explicit deep copy.
rule.rationale: Copy is implicit and zero-cost; Clone is explicit and signals "this may allocate". Mixing them confuses callers about copy cost.

rule.statement: Use Cow<'a, str> when a function sometimes modifies its input and sometimes returns it unchanged — avoids unconditional cloning.
rule.rationale: Cow::Borrowed is a zero-cost borrow; Cow::Owned is a heap allocation. The choice is deferred to runtime.

example.title: Cow for Conditional Cloning

rule.statement: Use move closures when the closure must outlive the current scope or be sent to another thread. Use borrows when the closure is called within the same scope.
rule.rationale: move captures all referenced variables by value; without move, the closure borrows them and cannot outlive the borrow.

antipattern.description: Adding .clone() every time the borrow checker complains without investigating why.
antipattern.reason: clone() allocates and copies; it is a band-aid over a design flaw in the ownership graph. Budget rule: if the same function signature has been changed 3+ times for borrow errors, simplify the design (return owned, restructure the data).

antipattern.description: Returning a reference to a local value (dangling reference).
antipattern.reason: The local is dropped at function exit; the reference points to freed memory. Compiler rejects it, but LLMs sometimes suggest workarounds like Box::leak — avoid.

antipattern.description: Using fn foo(s: &String) instead of fn foo(s: &str).
antipattern.reason: Clippy lint ptr_arg catches this. &String forces callers to own a String; &str accepts any string slice.

antipattern.description: Adding 'static to every lifetime parameter to silence "does not outlive 'static" errors.
antipattern.reason: Poisons the API — forces callers to own or leak data. Walk back to the original mismatch; usually data is borrowed when it should be owned.

section.name: Agent Workflow
section.p: A code-writing agent first answers three ownership questions: (1) does this function consume, borrow, or share its inputs? (2) does it return owned data or a reference into existing data? (3) does it cross a thread boundary (Send + Sync needed)? Answer is captured in the function signature before the body is written. A code-review agent runs cargo clippy (with clippy::needless_pass_by_value, clippy::redundant_clone, clippy::ptr_arg) and cargo machete to flag mis-ownership and dead clones. Borrow-check failures are treated as design feedback, not noise — never papered over with .clone().

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — runs the task; constitution.md should pin "no .clone() to silence borrow checker".
A purpose-built borrow-checker-explainer subagent — given a cargo build failure, expands the cryptic error into "what & to remove or what to consume".
A clippy-enforcer subagent — runs Clippy gates with a strict ownership-related allowlist.

section.name: Prompt Pattern
section.p: Before writing the body, answer: 1. Each parameter: borrowed (&T), mutably borrowed (&mut T), or owned (T)? 2. Return type: owned (T) or reference (&'a T) into a parameter? 3. Threads: is this called from spawn? If so, all captures must be 'static. Print the signature first, then the body.
This compile error: "<paste>". Walk me through the ownership graph of the function. Suggest the minimal fix that does not call .clone() or wrap in Rc<RefCell<T>>. If neither is possible, say so.

list.item: cargo — Build / test / borrow check
cargo clippy — Lints around ownership patterns
cargo expand — Inspect generated code; useful for macros that move values
cargo machete — Find unused deps after refactor
rust-analyzer — LSP — inlay hints show inferred lifetimes & types
bacon — Background cargo check watcher
mold / lld — Faster linker
miri — Detect undefined behavior in unsafe code
loom — Concurrency permutation tester
Rust Playground — Quick borrow-checker experiments via API.
godbolt.org (Compiler Explorer) — Inspect MIR / asm to verify zero-cost abstractions.
crates.io — Look up Send/Sync annotations of imported types.
Lib.rs — Same niche, often more accurate type info.
GitHub Copilot Workspace / Cursor — Partial: Borrow-checker errors propagate poorly; needs human escalation often.
API rule of thumb: take the most flexible type you can (&str over &String, &[T] over &Vec<T>, impl IntoIterator over Vec<T>).
Return owned data when the function allocates; return a borrow when slicing into a parameter.
Prefer iterator chains (.iter().filter().map().collect()) over indexing — borrow checker is happier and code is faster.
Use Cow<'a, str> when sometimes-borrowed sometimes-owned (parser output, normalization).
For shared state across threads, prefer message passing (mpsc, tokio::sync::mpsc) over Arc<Mutex<T>>.
Don't reach for Rc<RefCell<T>> to silence the borrow checker — usually means data layout is wrong (graph in a tree, etc.).
Lifetime elision: avoid explicit 'a parameters until the compiler asks; once it asks, name them after their meaning ('src, 'config).
For async, all spawned tasks need 'static — capture by value with move, or Arc::clone shared state.
Use rust-analyzer inlay hints to make ownership transfers visible while typing.
LLMs default to .clone() to escape the borrow checker. Override with: "If you call .clone(), justify in a comment why the borrow can't work. Otherwise rewrite the signature."
LLMs writing fn foo(s: &String) instead of &str — clippy lint catches; agent must accept the lint, not silence it.

LLMs love Rc<RefCell<T>> from web-search results; in async contexts they then write Arc<Mutex<T>> and forget tokio's Mutex is async-aware (std::sync::Mutex is fine in compute-heavy code, tokio::sync::Mutex for await while holding).

Agents accept "lifetime 'a may not outlive 'static" by adding 'static everywhere — this poisons APIs and cascades. Walk back to the original mismatch; usually the data is borrowed when it should be owned.
Self-referential structs: LLMs invent &self-referencing fields; safe Rust forbids. Agent must recognize and use Pin, ouroboros, or restructure.
Async iterators / streams: LLMs use .iter() inside async and hold borrows across .await, which breaks Send. Refactor to consume the iterator before the await point.
Closures capturing & while also &mut of the same value — the lint message is unhelpful; agents need to recognize the pattern (e.g., vec.iter().for_each(|x| vec.push(x.clone())) is illegal).
Borrow-checker fights waste tokens fast. Budget rule: if the agent has changed the same function signature 3+ times, escalate or simplify (collect into Vec, return owned, redesign).
The methodology covers basic ownership but not the deeper Pin, unsafe, or async-Send issues that bite in real apps.
Lifetime elision rules are presented as "compiler infers" — agents that paste examples then add a second reference parameter break the elision and don't know why.
Rc<RefCell<T>> and Arc<Mutex<T>> are introduced as escape hatches but the runtime cost (pointer chasing, lock contention, refcount atomics) is rarely quantified.
String vs &str advice is sound, but generic impl AsRef<str> is the more flexible choice for libs and is under-discussed.
Self-referential structs are flat-out unsupported by safe Rust; agents need ouroboros / pinning, neither is in basic ownership docs.
Borrow-checker errors are notoriously cryptic for agents — "lifetime 'a does not outlive 'b" doesn't explain which & to remove.

template.purpose: Canonical function signature patterns for common ownership shapes.
